title: Cranachan
subtitle: Toasted oats, whipped cream, whisky, honey, and fresh raspberries layered in a glass
excerpt: A Scottish dessert of toasted rolled oats, softly whipped cream flavoured with whisky and honey, and fresh raspberries layered in glasses. Each component is kept separate and layered at the last minute so the oats stay crisp and the cream stays cold. Sometimes called the pudding of Scotland.
sourceNotes: Cranachan is a traditional Scottish dessert with roots in harvest celebrations. Historically it was made with crowdie (a fresh Scottish cheese) rather than cream, but the cream version became standard over the twentieth century. It is strongly associated with Burns Night (25 January) and summer raspberry season across Scotland.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Toast the oats and prepare the cream mixture up to 4 hours ahead. Layer with the raspberries just before serving, assemble at the table for the full effect.

content.text: Cranachan is a layered dessert, not a mixed one. The components stay separate in the glass so that each spoonful contains all three elements: crisp oat, cold whisky-scented cream, and sharp fresh raspberry. If everything is stirred together it becomes a pleasant but undifferentiated mush. Layer with intention.

The whisky in the cream is not there for alcohol content but for flavour, it is stirred in when the cream is soft, and its warmth works against the cold, sweet cream and the sharp raspberry. Use a good Scotch malt; a blended whisky is fine but a single malt will be noticeably better. Add more or less to your preference, this is a recipe where the cook's judgement matters more than an exact measure.
Toast {{rolled-oats}} of rolled oats in a dry frying pan over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 3, 5 minutes until golden and smelling nutty. Tip onto a plate and cool completely.
Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks. Stir in {{whisky}} of whisky and {{honey}} of honey. Taste and adjust, more honey if you want it sweeter, more whisky if you want more warmth.

In each glass, put a layer of {{raspberries}} of raspberries, then a spoonful of cream, then a scatter of toasted oats. Repeat the layers, finishing with oats and a few raspberries on top. Serve immediately so the oats stay crisp.

items.cause: Assembled too far ahead, or the cream was warm when assembled
items.fix: Assemble cranachan at the last moment, no more than 15 minutes before serving. The cream must be cold from the fridge.

items.symptom: Cream has a slightly bitter taste
items.cause: Too much whisky, or the whisky chosen was too peaty
items.fix: Start with 30 ml of whisky and taste before adding more. Heavily peated whiskies (Islay malts) can overwhelm the cream. A sweeter malt (Speyside) works better in this dessert.
Start with 2 tablespoons and taste. The raspberries should provide the acidity that offsets the sweetness.

items.symptom: Raspberries have made the cream pink and liquid
items.cause: Very ripe berries releasing juice into the cream layers
items.fix: Use raspberries that are ripe but firm. Assemble just before serving. If the berries are very ripe, press half into the base and leave the rest whole on top.

content.text: Cranachan is a Scottish dessert with roots in the harvest supper traditions of the Highlands, where a mixture of toasted oatmeal, cream, and wild raspberries was made at the time of the berry harvest. The original dish used crowdie, a traditional Scottish fresh cheese, in place of cream, and the modern cream version is a relatively recent standardisation. The dessert is strongly associated with Burns Night (25 January) celebrations, where it is often served at the end of the meal, and with summer when Scottish raspberries are at their peak.
